# MAIN ARTICLE Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical j o u r nal homep age: www.elsevier.com/locate/snb # A facile preparation of montmorillonite-supported copper sulfide nanocomposites and their application in the detection of H2O2 Leyou Zhang, Mingxing Chen, Yanling Jiang, Miaomiao Chen, Yanan Ding, Qingyun Liu ∗ School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao266590, PR China # a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received20April2016Received in revised form28July2016Accepted29July2016Available online30July2016 Keywords: Montmorillonite CuS Nanocomposites Peroxidase H2O2 Colorimetric # 1. Introduction With the emergence and recent advances of nanoscience and nanotechnology, the design and construction of enzyme mimics have been offered new opportunities [1]. Owing to possessing excellent properties of natural enzymes regarding high efficiency, high specificity as well as more remarkable advantages such as low cost, less vulnerable to denaturation with environmental changes, easy preparation and purification, greater resistance to extremes of acidity, temperature and inhibitors [2-5], enzyme mimics have been an increasingly important focus for the scientists in recent years. More interestingly, Gao et al. [6] have reported that Fe3O4nanoparticles (NPs) have an intrinsic enzyme mimetic activity similar to that of naturally occurring HRP, thus paving the way for the application of nanomaterials in the field of catalysis. From then on, enzyme mimetics of HRP nanomaterial-based have become an active field of scientific research. In the past few years, various kinds of nanomaterials have been explored to possess enzyme mimetic activity such as polymer-coated CeO2NPs [7], Co3O4NPs ∗ Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: qyliu@sdust.edu.cn, qyliusdu@163.com (Q. Liu). # a b s t r a c t Well-dispersed nanocomposites (CuS-MMT nanocomposites) based on copper sulfide (CuS) and Camontmorillonite (Ca2+ -MMT) were prepared via a simple and facile method and were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The as-prepared CuS-MMT NPs were for the first time demonstrated to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity towards typical peroxidase substrates such as3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Interestingly, the CuS-MMT nanocomposites as peroxidase mimics could rapidly catalyse to oxidise the substrate TMB into blue oxTMB only in30s observed by the naked eye. The reaction catalyzed by the CuS-MMT nanocomposites followed the Michaelis-Menten kinetics, which showed good affinity towards both TMB and H2O2, respectively. Taking the advantage of the high peroxidase-like activity, the as-prepared CuS-MMT nanocomposites were designed to establish a novel colorimetric sensor for H2O2with a wide linear range from3 × 10 -5to2 × 10 -4M. Moreover, the rapid, simple and sensitive TMB-H2O2-[CuS-MMT] colorimetric platform holds great promising applications in the pharmaceutical, clinical and industrial detection of H2O2. © 2016Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. [8], V2O5nanowires [9], BiFeO3NPs [10], etc, which could function as enzymatic mimics. Copper sulfide (CuS), as a p type semiconductor with a direct band gap of1.2 -2.0eV [11] and an important transition metal sulfide, has attracted tremendous interest. Hence, it has widely applied in photocatalysis [12], solar cell devices [13], optical limiting [14], biosensors [15] and Li-ion batteries [16], due to the typical optical, electronic, physical and chemical properties. To date, various CuS nanomaterials have been utilized in the fabrication of biosensors, due to its inexpensive, nontoxic, easily produced and readily stored [17]. However, as well known, nanoparticles easily aggregate or precipitate in the aqueous solution due to their high specific surface energy, which seriously hinders the catalytic performance [18]. Thus, it is obvious that further efforts to develop new well-dispersed peroxidase mimetics are still required to underway and hold significant incentives in the field of biochemistry analysis. Montmorillonite (MMT) has been a research focus in many fields such as materials chemistry, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, regenerative medicine, etc. [19], because of its low cost and eco-friendliness, as well as its unique properties such as high surface reactivity (high adsorption, cation exchange, colloidal or swelling capacity), good rheological behavior, high acid-absorbing capacity, and high dispersibility in water [20]. Hybrid composites with well-defined structures have been extensively explored to realize the combination of the respective properties of each component or to achieve cooperatively enhanced performances [21]. Up to now, various nanoparticles have been prepared on the basis of montmorillonite such as Cu, Ag, Pd, Pt, Co [22-25], TiO2, ZnO [26-28] and Al2O3 [29], which have been proved to possess enhanced properties that cannot be realized by either component alone. However, there is no any report on the application of MMT-based nanocomposites for the sensitive and visual colorimetric detection of H2O2. Therefore, considering obvious superiority of copper sulfide and montmorillonite individually, the peroxidase-like activity of CuS-MMT composites from synergetic effect was expected to produce when integrating copper sulfide and montmorillonite into nanocomposites. Herein, we employed a simple and facile method to prepare copper sulfide-decorated Ca-montmorillonite (CuS-MMT) nanocomposites. The CuS nanoparticles were formed and adsorbed on the Ca-montmorillonite surface without aggregation. The asprepared CuS-MMT NPs were for the first time demonstrated to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity towards typical peroxidase substrates such as3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Interestingly, the CuS-MMT nanocomposites as peroxidase mimics could rapidly catalyse the oxidation of the substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2only in30s into a blue product observed by the naked eye. The as-prepared CuS-MMT nanocomposites with the advantages of both Ca-montmorillonite and CuS were used as new nanocatalysts to construct a facile, sensitive and colorimetric H2O2sensor. The developed method exhibited rapid and sensitive response toward H2O2detection. # 2. Experimental section # 2.1. Materials CuCl2 · 6H2O, hydrogen peroxide (30% wt%, H2O2), acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc) CuSO4 · 5H2O, NaNO3, glucose, and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Guangcheng Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). FeCl3 · 6H2O was purchased from Beichenfangzheng Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Solarbio (Tianjin, China). Thioacetamide was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All the reagents above were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. De-ionized water was used throughout the experiment. # 2.2. Characterization The morphology and shape of the nanoparticles were obtained by the transmission electron microscope and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) with an accelerating voltage of200kV (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The crystal structures of the products were recorded on powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with graphite monochromatized Cu K ɑ radiation (D/Max2500PC, Rigaku). The elemental composition of the complex was identified by using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on a MAPADA UV-3200PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker ESR300E with a microwave bridge (receiver gain, 1 × 10 5 ; modulation amplitude, 2G; microwave power, 10mW; modulation frequency, 100kHz). The photoluminescence spectra were obtained using an F-4600FLSPECTOROPHOTOMET spectrofluorophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Science Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The zeta potential measurements were performed on an electrophoresis apparatus (JS94H, China). # 2.3. Synthesis of CuS-MMT nanocomposites CuS-MMT nanocomposites were prepared as follows: 0.3g of MMT was dissolved in30mL distilled water under stirring violently for18h to form a homogeneous suspension. Then, 5mL of the CuCl2aqueous solution (0.15mmol) were dropwise added into the above suspension under vigorous stirring for another3h. The resulting solution was assigned as solution A. Subsequently, 9mL HCl solution (0.5M) was added into another beaker containing0.169g of thioacetamide (assigned as solution B). Then solutions A and B were immediately put into a sealed container under vigorous stirring for24h. The final products were separated by centrifugation, washed with pure water for three times and dried at60 ◦ C for8h in the air. # 2.4. Catalytic oxidation test of TMB by CuS-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites was investigated by measuring the catalytic oxidation of the substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. In a typical procedure, 200 𝛍 L of TMB (1mM) in sodium acetate buffer solution (pH3.8) was subsequently treated with (a) 200 𝛍 L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and CuS-MMT NPs (200 𝛍 L, 0.3mg mL -1 ), (b) 200 𝛍 L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and CuS NPs (200 𝛍 L, 0.3mg mL -1 ), (c) 200 𝛍 L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and MMT NPs (200 𝛍 L, 0.3mg mL -1 ), (d) 200 𝛍 L of30% H2O2 (0.25M), (e) 200 𝛍 L of CuS-MMT NPs (0.3mg mL -1 ), and (f) no addition of H2O2and CuS-MMT NPs, respectively. The total reaction volume is2mL. The different experimental systems was measured by recording the absorbance data in time scan mode at629nm. # 2.5. Kinetic measurements The kinetic measurement was carried out in time-scan mode by monitoring the absorbance evolutions at629nm. The reaction was carried out using200 𝛍 L CuS-MMT nanocomposites as enzyme mimics (0.3mg mL -1 ) in sodium acetate buffer solution (1.4mL, pH3.8) with H2O2or TMB as substrate, respectively. The experiment with TMB as the substrate was performed using the CuS-MMT nanocomposites (0.3mg mL -1 ) and fixed concentration of H2O2 (0.25M) constant while varying concentration of TMB (0.01-0.09mM). Similarly, the experiment with H2O2as the substrate was performed using the CuS-MMT nanocomposites (0.3mg mL -1 ) with varying concentrations of H2O2 (0.001-10mM) while keeping the concentration of TMB (1mM) constant. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) was calculated by fitting the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/ /GS = Km/Vmax (1/[S] + 1/Km), where v is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of the substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant [21]. # 2.6. Colorimetric detection of H2O2using CuS-MMT nanocomposites H2O2detection was carried out as follows: firstly, 200 𝛍 L of H2O2with different concentrations (0.002-0.8mM) and200 𝛍 L of CuS-MMT solution (0.3mg mL -1 ) were added into1.4mL sodium acetate buffer solution (pH3.8). Then, 200 𝛍 L of TMB (1mM) was added to the above solution and the obtained mixed solution was incubated for5min. Finally, the absorbance data of the resulting solution were recorded on a UV-vis spectrophotometer. In order to investigate the selectivity of CuS-MMT, 20.0mM of Fe3+ , Cu2+ , NO3 -, glucose, and citric acid (CA) were added as the control experiments, respectively. Fig. 1. The XRD pattern of pure CuS (A), CuS-MMT NPs (B) and MMT (C), respectively. Fig. 2. The EDS pattern of CuS-MMT NPs. # 2.7. Reusability of the CuS-MMT as peroxidase mimetics The reusability of CuS-MMT nanoparticles was measured after five successive repeated cycles in aforementioned reaction system. After reacting for5min, the reaction system was centrifuged for10min before measuring the absorbance at629nm. The recycled nanomaterials was washed several times with deionized water and was reused for the next cycle. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1. Characterization of CuS-MMT nanopartcles Fig. 1shows the XRD patterns of CuS, CuS-MMT nanocomposites and MMT, respectively. The XRD pattern of as-prepared CuS sample (Fig. 1A) shows several diffraction features, agreeing well with reported pure hexagonal (covellite) CuS system (JCPDS card No. 06-0464). From Fig. 1B, characteristic peak at2 𝛉 = 48.107 ◦ ( * ) was obviously observed which was attributed to (110) plane for CuS alone, suggesting the formation of the CuS nanoparticles on the surface of MMT. The reason why only a characteristic peak was observed in the CuS-MMT nanocomposites was the less amount of CuS loaded in composites together with the disturbance of MMT lamellar structures. EDS analysis was further used to discern the chemical components from the CuS-MMT nanocomposites. Fig. 2presents a representative EDS analysis of the CuS-MMT nnaocomposites. The dominant peaks of Fe, Al and Si as well as weak peaks of Mg and Ca appear in the spectrum, which origin from the montmorillonite materials. Additionally, from Fig. 2, two obvious Cu and S peaks can also be found, suggesting that CuS were successfully fab-

Table1Comparison of the reaction time required for colorimetric visualization. ricated onto MMT materials. Moreover, the molar ratio of Cu and S was detected to be ca. 1.14, which approximately corresponds to the theoretical value1. The morphology of CuS-MMT nanocomposites was imaged by TEM, shown in Fig. 3. TEM images (Fig. 3A) show that the asprepared CuS nanoparticles with an average size of approximately38nm were dispersed on the surface of MMT, which was verified by comparing the interlamellar spacing (001lattice plane) of MMT before and after decorated with CuS nanoparticles. It is found that the interlamellar spacings of MMT and CuS-MMT were almost consistent (approximately1.50nm, (001) lattice plane), providing a direct evidence that CuS nanoparticles were dispersed on the surface of MMT, shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). Fig. 3B is the high resolution TEM. From this figure, it can be seen that the lattice space of the CuS nanoparticles is ca. 0.282nm, which corresponds to (103) plane of hexagonal CuS crystal. The stability of the nanocomposites in the aqueous solution was confirmed by zeta potentials ( -39.1mV). The high value of zeta potentials indicated CuS-MMT nanocomposite could be stably dispersed in aqueous solution for a relative long time. Besides, it also could be seen that the CuS-MMT nanocomposites showed a good stability in1.5h (Fig. S2, Supporting information). # 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of CuS-MMT NPs The catalytic performance of as-prepared CuS-MMT nanocomposites was tested by using the TMB, a typical peroxidase substrate, in the presence of H2O2. Interestingly, after CuS-MMT nanocomposites had been added into the TMB-H2O2system for only30second s, an obvious blue change was observed by the naked eye, indicating that the substrate TMB was oxidized rapidly. The rapid color reaction was also monitored by the UV-vis absorption spectroscopy with the increase of time, shown in Fig. 4A. The result was further confirmed by the control experiments. As shown in Fig. 4B, a deeper colour was produced in the presence of CuS-MMT nanocomposites (Fig. 4B-a) than that of either CuS (Fig. 4B-b) or MMT (Fig. 4B-c) individual, indicating the higher catalytic activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites. In addition, the TMB-H2O2solution without addition of CuS-MMT NPs or the TMBCuS-MMT solution in the absence of H2O2exhibits negligible color change, suggesting that both the CuS-MMT NPs and H2O2were required for the catalytic reaction. These results confirmed that the CuS-MMT nanocomposites exhibited the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. Moreover, Table1shows the comparison of the reaction time required for colorimetric visualization. From the data listed in Table1, it can be seen that the substrate TMB is oxidized rapidly owing to adding of CuS-MMT nanocomposites and visualized the color change by the naked eye in the shortest time, exhibiting the highest catalytic activity of the nanocomposites, compared with other materials listed in Table1. # 3.3. Optimization of experimental conditions Similar to some peroxidase mimics as well as natural peroxidase HRP, the catalytic activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites also Fig. 3. The TEM image (A) and the HRTEM image (B) of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites. Fig. 4. (A) Time dependent UV-vis spectra of TMB-H2O2system catalyzed by CuS-MMT NPs. Concentration of TMB: 1mM; H2O2: 0.25M; CuS-MMT: 0.3mg mL -1 . (B) UV-vis absorption-time course curve of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) using CuS-MMT NPs at629nm under different conditions: (a) TMB + CuS-MMT + H2O2, (b) TMB + CuS+ H2O2, (c) TMB + MMT+ H2O2, (d) TMB + H2O2, (e) TMB + CuS-MMT, and (f) TMB, respectively. Fig. 5. Influences of (A) pH and (B) temperature on the catalytic oxidation of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of CuS-MMT NPs and H2O2. The maximum point in each curve (A-B) was set as100%. depends on pH, temperature and size, respectively. To explore the effects of pH and temperature on the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by CuS-MMT nanocomposites in the presence of H2O2, the pH experiments were performed by using the buffer solution with different pH values (2.6, 3.8, 5, 5.7, 6, 7, 8.7, 9.9). As shown in Fig. 5A, the maximum absorbance was obtained when pH value was approximately pH3.8. However, the catalytic activity of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites was lower than that of pH3.8in relatively strong acidic or alkaline solution [8]. Thus, pH3.8was selected for the following assay. Moreover, the effect of temperature-dependent on the catalytic activity of nanocomposites was also investigated over a wide temperature range from25to60 ◦ C. From Fig. 5B, it can be seen that the catalytic activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites increased gradually with increasing of the experimental temperatures, which was different from the HRP reported in the previous publication [6]. According to the previous report [6], the activity of HRP would gradually decrease from35 ◦ C, and even lost completely at a high temperature. In contrast, the catalytic activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites increased with increasing experimental temperatures. Therefore, the results indicated that the as-prepared Fig. 6. Steady-state kinetic assay of the CuS-MMT. (A-F): The velocity of the reaction was measured in1.4mL NaOAc buffer (pH3.8) containing0.3mg mL -1of CuS-MMT. (A and C): The concentration of TMB was1mM while the H2O2was varied, (B and D): The concentration of H2O2was0.25M while the TMB was varied, (E and F): Double reciprocal plots of the activity of CuS-MMT at a fixed concentration of one substrate versus varying concentration of the second substrate for H2O2and TMB. CuS-MMT nanocomposites were resistant to higher temperature. In addition, the activity of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites is also dependent on the size of the CuS particles deposited on the surface of MMT. In order to comparatively investigate the catalytic activity of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites with different size, we prepared some CuS-MMT composites with larger size (ca. 209nm), shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting information). Fig. S4 (Supporting information) shows the catalytic activity of CuS-MMT with different size after5min. From Fig. S4, it can be seen that the smaller the CuS nanoparticls deposited on MMT, the higher the catalytic activity. # 3.4. Steady-state kinetic assay of CuS-MMT NPs As we know, the catalytic oxidation of TMB by many enzymes mimetics follows the Michaelis-Menten behaviors. In order to get a better insight into the peroxidase-like activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites, a series of experiments are performed by varying the concentration of one substrate and keeping the concentration of the other constant. Typical Michaelis-Menten curves shown in Fig. 6for TMB and H2O2are obtained by plotting the initial reaction rate against concentration. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) indicating the affinity of an enzyme for its substrate is obtained by using the Lineweaver-Burk (L-B) plot. The smaller the value of Km, the stronger the affinity between the catalysts and the substrate. As illustrated in Table2, The Km value for CuSMMT nanocomoposites with TMB as the substrate is calculated to be0.0212mM, which was20times lower than that for HRP enzyme, suggesting the better binding affinity of CuS-MMT to TMB than that of HRP [6]. Similarly, the Km value of CuS-MMT with H2O2as substrate is2.27mM, slightly lower than that of HRP [6]. In addition, the catalytic mechanism of CuS-MMT was pursued through the analysis of Lineweaver -Burk plots over a range

The effect of the amount of CuS-MMT on the formation of hydroxyl radicals using terephthalic acid as a fluorescence probe. Reaction conditions: H2O2 (0.25M), terephthalicacid (0.5mM) and the CuS-MMT nanocomposites with different concentration ((A) 0, (B) 0.3, (C) 0.5, (D) 0.6, (E) 0.8, (F) 1.0mg mL -1 ) were incubated in acetate buffer (pH3.8, 200mM) at40 ◦ C for40min. The FL excitation wavelength was fixed at315nm. of TMB and H2O2concentrations. Fig. 6E and F showed double reciprocal plots of initial velocity against the concentration of one substrate in a range of concentrations of the second substrate. These lines which are approximately parallel are the characteristic of a ping-pong mechanism, as observed for HRP, Fe3O4@C NPs, Pt NPs/rGO, C-dot/NiAl-LDH, FeVO4-4NBs [6,34]. The result indicated that CuS-MMT bound and reacted with the first substrate and then released the first product before reacting with the second substrate [6,21,34]. # 3.5. Mechanism of catalytic activity of CuS-MMT The nature of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites as peroxidase mimetics may originate from the production of hydroxyl radicals (OH · ) due to the decomposition of H2O2. To confirm this assumption, the terephthalic acid (TA) photoluminescence probing technique is used to detect hydroxyl radical catalytically produced in the reaction by CuS-MMT nanocomposites [35], because nonluminescent terephthalic acid (TA) easily reacted with OH · to form2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (HTA) with strongly fluorescent signal [36]. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence signal intensity increased gradually with increasing of the concentration of the CuSMMT nanocomposites, indicating that the amount of produced OH · radicals increased with the increase of the amount of CuS-MMT nanocomposites. The result further suggested H2O2was actually decomposed into OH · radicals catalyzed by CuS-MMT nanocomposites, which was well in agreement with the behaviors of the CoFe2O4NPs, ZnFe2O4MNPs and H2TCPP-NiO NPs reported in previous publications [37,38]. In addition, the formation of OH · radicals from decomposition of H2O2in the presence of CuS-MMT peroxidase catalysis was further Fig. 8. spin-trapping ESR spectra of · OH radicals in the systems of (A) H2O2-DMPO, (B) MMT-H2O2-DMPO and (C) CuS-MMT NPs-H2O2-DMPO. Reaction conditions: 18mM DMPO, 25mM H2O2, 28.2mg L -1MMT, 28.2mg L -1CuS-MMT NPs. confirmed by the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. 5,5dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was adopted as a specific target molecule to capture OH · radicals. It was obviously observed that the DMPO/ · OH adduct signal intensity of the ESR spectra in the presence of the CuS-MMT nanocomposites was stronger than that of the ESR spectra of MMT alone or that of the ESR spectra in the absence of CuS-MMT nanocomposites, shown in Fig. 8. This result provides a direct evidence of the nanoenzyme-mimic activity of CuS-MMT nanocomposites. # 3.6. The application of CuS-MMT in the colorimetric sensing of H2O2 On the basis of the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of CuSMMT NPs, a simple colorimetric method for H2O2detection was developed. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the typical absorbance at629nm versus various concentrations of H2O2response curve was plotted in Fig. 9A. Obviously, it can be seen that the absorbance of oxidized TMB gradually increases with the increasing of H2O2concentration ranged from2to800 𝛍 M. Fig. 9B illustrates that the absorbance at629nm of oxidized TMB is proportional to the H2O2concentration ranged from30to200 𝛍 M with a detection limit of2.47 × 10 -5M. In comparion with CuZnFeS [39] and CuS-graphene [40] -based colorimertic sensor using TMB as substrate, the CuS-MMT NPs exhibited a much wider detection range. Moreover, the detection limit is comparable with that of previous work based on Au@Pt core-shell nanorods (4.5 × 10 -5M) [41], indicating the CuS-MMT NPs as mimic enzyme are sensitive. # 3.7. Selectivity analysis The selectivity of the CuS-MMT-based optical detection system was evaluated by the absorbance change in the presence of several potentially interfering substances. 20.0mM of Fe3+ , Cu2+ , NO3 -, glucose, and citric acid (CA) were injected detection system and tested with the experimental procedure as mentioned above. It can be seen from Fig. 10(A) that no obvious absorbance or color change was observed even when the concentrations of interferences were10times higher than that of standard H2O2 (2.0mM), Therefore, the results demonstrated the high specificity of CuS-MMT for H2O2detection. Fig. 9. (A) A dose-response curve for H2O2detection using CuS-MMT as catalysts. (B) The linear calibration plot for H2O2detection. Error bars represent the standard deviation based on three repeated measurements. # 3.8. Reusability of the CuS-MMT The reusability of CuS-MMT as a peroxidase nanomimetic was investigated by performing the circulating catalytic experiments. After each cycle, the CuS-MMT samples were collected and reused in next cycle. From Fig. 11, it can be seen that the catalytic activity of CuS-MMT slightly decreases with the increase of cycle times, but it still retains approximately80% after5catalytic cycles, irrespective of the loss of the nanocomposites in each cycle. The result indicated that CuS-MMT nanocomposites exhibited an excellent reusability during the catalytic reaction. # 4. Conclusion In summary, the CuS-MMT nanocomposites was prepared by a facile one-step approach and for the first time demonstrated to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The CuS-MMT nanocomposites could rapidly catalyse the oxidation of the substrate TMB only in30s observed by the naked eye. A series of assays indicate that the catalysis follows typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and its catalytic property is strongly dependent on pH and temperature. Based on this finding, we have successfully Fig. 10. (A) Selectivity analysis for CuS-MMT for2mM H2O2in comparison to20.0mM of Fe3+ , Cu2+ , NO3 -, glucose, and citric acid (CA) (Inset: related color changes). Fig. 11. Reusability of catalytic activity of CuS-MMT NPs. established a simple, sensitive colorimetric and visual assay for the detection of H2O2. The inherent catalytic properties of the CuS-MMT NPs may hold potential applications in biosensors, immunoassays, biocatalysis and environmental monitoring. # Acknowledgements This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant no. 21271119), Shandong University of Science & Technology is gratefully acknowledged. # Appendix A. RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 17483-17493. # Biographies Leyou Zhang is currently working toward the master degree under the supervision of Prof. Qingyun Liu in the College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering at Shandong University of Science and Technology, majoring in Applied Chemistry. And his research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial-based sensors. Mingxing Chen received his B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from Shandong University of Science and Technology in2015. Currently, he is a graduate student in Shandong University of Science and Technology, majoring in Applied Chemistry. His major research arrears focus on the preparation of enzyme mimics and their sensing application. Miaomiao Chen received her B.E. degree from Shandong University of Science and Technology in2015. Currently, she is a graduate student in Shandong University of Science and Technology, majoring in Applied Chemistry. Her major research interests focus on the preparation of enzyme mimics and their sensing application. Yanan Ding received her B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from University of Jinan in2015. Currently, she is studying for master degree under the supervision of Prof. Qingyun Liu in Shandong University of Science and Technology, majoring in Applied Chemistry. Her research interest includes the preparation of enzyme mimics and their sensing application. Prof. Qingyun Liu received his PhD degree from Shandong University, China. Following postdoctoral research in Ocean University of China, she successively joined Prof. Yansheng Yin group. Now she is a professor at Shandong University of Science & Technology, China. Her research interests include inorgainic-organic nanocomposites, colorimetric sensor, self-assembly of porphyrin and phthalocyanine.
